entry.title: On-Premises to Cloud SAP S/4HANA Conversion Planning and Execution – Part 1: Migration Timeline etc.
entry.content:
1. Introduction: Why Scheduling and Downtime Optimization Matter

The success of an SAP migration depends heavily on how well you plan the schedule and minimize downtime. Business processes such as finance (FI), sales (SD), and material management (MM) cannot afford extended outages when you have a narrow window, such as a 48-hour weekend; every minute counts. The goal is to perform as many tasks as possible while the system is still live so that during the actual downtime, you only need to do the final steps.
This article explains how to achieve this by combining SAP tools with storage replication technology, helping you make a smooth transition with minimal interruption to your business.

2. Understanding Migration Approaches

2.1 Greenfield, Brownfield, and Hybrid Approaches

There are three main approaches to move from ECC to S/4HANA:

- Greenfield (New Implementation): Build a new S/4HANA system in the cloud from scratch. You re-implement business processes and migrate only the data you need. This method gives you a clean start but requires rebuilding many processes. It usually means a short downtime only for the final data load.

- Brownfield (System Conversion): Convert your existing ECC system into S/4HANA. You keep your current data and customizations. However, this approach can result in a long downtime unless you optimize it using SAP tools.

- Hybrid (Selective Data Transition or Bluefield): Combine elements of both approaches. You keep parts of your ECC system and re-implement others. This method is flexible but more complex.

2.2 How Each Approach Affects Downtime

- Greenfield: Downtime is usually limited to the final cutover because the new system is built in parallel.

- Brownfield: The system conversion happens in one big step, which can result in long downtime if not optimized.

- Hybrid: A Hybrid approach often involves selective data transition: you extract data from the old system and import into a new S/4HANA system in a controlled way, possibly using specialized tools. You might keep certain historical data or configurations and drop others.

3. Preparing for Migration

3.1 System Cleanup and Data Archiving

- Clean up Data: Archive or delete outdated data to reduce the amount of information that needs to be migrated.

- Custom Code Review: Use tools like the ABAP Test Cockpit to identify and fix issues in custom code. In one project, cleaning up historical data reduced migration time by 30%.

3.2 Provisioning the Cloud Environment

- Infrastructure as Code (IaC): Tools like Terraform can quickly create a cloud environment with the same settings.

- Pre-Installation: Install the S/4HANA software and perform basic configuration on the cloud environment ahead of the cutover. This includes setting up necessary services, preparing the operating system, and verifying network connectivity.

3.3 Pre-Copying Data with NetApp SnapMirror

NetApp SnapMirror allows you to replicate on-premises data to the cloud before cutover:

- Initial Baseline: Transfer a full copy of critical data volumes (database files, SAP Global Directory, transport directory) from on-premise NetApp storage to your cloud storage (e.g., AWS FSx for ONTAP, Azure NetApp Files, or Google Cloud NetApp Volumes).

- Incremental Updates: Schedule hourly or more frequent updates so that by the time you enter the downtime window, only the final changes need to be transferred.

- Cutover Sync: During the migration weekend, trigger a final SnapMirror update. Once the update is complete, break the SnapMirror relationship to make the cloud volume writable.

Example Configuration:

 

 

# Create destination volume on the cloud NetApp system
cluster::> volume create -vserver CloudSVM -volume PRD_HANA_Data_Replica -aggregate aggr1 -size 5TB -type DP

# Establish SnapMirror relationship
cluster::> snapmirror create -source-path OnPremSVM:PRD_HANA_Data -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_Data_Replica -type XDP -schedule hourly

# Initialize SnapMirror
cluster::> snapmirror initialize -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_Data_Replica

# At cutover, run a final update and break the mirror
cluster::> snapmirror update -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_Data_Replica
cluster::> snapmirror break -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_Data_Replica

 

 

This process ensures that the final data transfer on the cutover weekend is limited to just the changes since the last incremental update, thereby dramatically reducing downtime.

4. SAP Standard Tools for Downtime Optimization

4.1 Software Update Manager (SUM) with Database Migration Option (DMO)

SUM DMO is the main tool for converting ECC to S/4HANA:

- Unified Process: It consolidates the upgrade and database migration steps into one process, reducing the overall downtime.

- Downtime-Optimized Mode: In this mode, a significant portion of the data conversion is performed while the system is still online. Only the final delta (the last set of changes) is applied during downtime. This is critical when you aim to meet the 48-hour window.

Example Command (Simplified):

 

 

# Start SUM in downtime-optimized mode on the source system
$ cd /usr/sap/<SID>/SUM
$ ./STARTUP confighostagent SID=<SID> scenario=DMO targetdbtype=HDB suspendmove=true

 

 

Later, when ready to complete on the target system:

 

 

$ cd /usr/sap/<SID>/SUM
$ ./RESUME systemmove

 

 

(Actual commands vary by SUM version; refer to your project’s documentation for specifics.)

4.2 Near-Zero Downtime Maintenance (nZDM)

Near-Zero Downtime Maintenance (nZDM) is a methodology designed to capture and replay transactional changes during a system conversion:

- Data Capture: Database triggers or replication capture changes while the system is online.

- Delta Replay: At cutover, after shutting down the source, the final changes (delta) are applied to the target S/4HANA system in a very short time.

- Result: This approach can reduce the actual downtime to just a few hours, even for large systems.

4.3 Integrating Tools for Optimal Downtime Reduction

By combining the use of SAP’s standard tools with NetApp SnapMirror, you can:

- Pre-copy almost all data to the cloud, so only a final delta must be transferred.

- Use SUM DMO in downtime-optimized mode to perform much of the conversion while the system is live.

- To further minimize downtime, consider adding an nZDM (Near Zero Downtime Migration) step, which quickly captures recent updates and replays them in close to real-time.

Completing these steps before moving to a new country will reduce the amount of work that needs to be done during the time when you are not working. During the 48-hour period, you should focus on finishing any final changes and the actual switch, not on the long tasks that prepare for the move.

5. Planning Your Cutover Weekend

A well-structured timeline is essential to meet the 48-hour target. Here is a sample plan:

5.1 Pre-Cutover Preparations (Weeks/Days Before)

- Clean up Data: Archive unnecessary data and resolve custom code issues.

- Provision the Cloud Environment: Use IaC (e.g., Terraform) to create your target S/4HANA system.

- Replicate Data: Use NetApp SnapMirror to pre-copy data from your on-premises system to the cloud. Schedule frequent incremental updates.

- Conduct Dry Runs: Perform at least two full migration rehearsals to measure and refine timings in a test environment.

5.2 Cutover Execution (During the 48-Hour Window)

Friday Evening:

- Freeze Users: Notify all users that the system will go offline and stop all transactions.

- Final Data Sync: Run SnapMirror to transfer the last delta changes. Break the SnapMirror relationship to make the cloud volumes writable.

- Stop ECC: Shut down the ECC system completely.

Friday Late Evening:

- Initiate SUM DMO Conversion: Start the conversion process on the target S/4HANA environment.

- Monitor in Real-Time: Basis administrators should closely monitor the logs and performance metrics during the conversion.

Saturday:

- Conversion Process: SUM DMO runs through its phases (export, import, conversion). If using nZDM, capture any last-minute changes.

- Technical and Functional Testing: Run initial tests to ensure critical transactions and reports work as expected.

Saturday Late/Early Sunday:

- Finalize Delta Sync (if using nZDM): Stop the production system and apply any final delta changes.

- Switch Over: Redirect interfaces, update DNS entries, and prepare for business resumption.

- Post-Migration Validation: Conduct system checks (using SAP’s ST03N, ST06, EarlyWatch Alert) to ensure data consistency and system performance.

5.3 Post-Cutover Activities

- Verification: Ask the people involved in your business to quickly check that your most important business processes are working correctly.

- Backup & DR: Establish new backup routines and disaster recovery procedures for the S/4HANA system.

- Support Setup: Make certain that, once the cutover is complete, your support teams stay on alert during the next business day to address problems as they appear.

6. Risk Management and Fallback Strategies

Even with the best preparation, things can go wrong. Thus, establishing a robust fallback plan is critical:

6.1 Go/No-Go Decision Points

Define clear criteria for proceeding with the cutover. For example, if a critical conversion step does not complete within a predetermined time (e.g., if the final delta sync exceeds 2 hours), the team should have the authority to call a “No-Go” and revert to the previous backup of ECC.

6.2 Rollback Plan

We recommend maintaining a recent backup of the ECC system so that if any critical issues arise during the migration, you can always revert to the old system. Using SnapMirror, you can also keep the on-premises system in a standby state until you are completely satisfied with the new system.

6.3 Escalation and Communication

Set up a clear way to talk about things, with regular updates on what's going on and clear steps to take if something goes wrong, making sure problems are fixed quickly and everyone's in the loop.

7. Post-Migration Optimization and Continuous Improvement

The moment the new S/4HANA setup is running, it’s important to remember that your responsibilities continue.

7.1 Performance Verification

Right after the system goes live, make sure to assess performance using SAP tools like ST03N, ST06, and EarlyWatch Alert, as well as any cloud-based monitoring solutions, then compare those findings to your pre-migration benchmarks and correct any issues you encounter.

7.2 Backup and Disaster Recovery

We know that data backup and disaster recovery are a big deal. That's why we'd like to suggest a cloud-centric plan to help you out. With tools like SnapMirror, you can rest easy knowing your data is safely stored somewhere else. This way, you'll never have to worry about losing your files, and you'll have consistent access to your information at all times.

7.3 FinOps and Cost Management

We're here to help you keep a close eye on how your resources are being used. We know it's important to use cost management tools that your cloud provider offers. We're also here to support you in using FinOps practices to make adjustments to your resource allocation. This will help you avoid overspending.

8. Technical Examples and Configuration Snippets

Below are examples of commands and configurations that illustrate key parts of the process:

8.1 NetApp SnapMirror Configuration Example

 

 

# On the cloud NetApp cluster, create a destination volume:
cluster::> volume create -vserver CloudSVM -volume PRD_HANA_data_replica -aggregate aggr1 -size 5TB -type DP

# On the on-premises NetApp cluster, establish the SnapMirror relationship:
cluster::> snapmirror create -source-path OnPremSVM:PRD_HANA_data -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_data_replica -type XDP -schedule hourly

# Initialize the SnapMirror relationship:
cluster::> snapmirror initialize -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_data_replica

# At cutover, perform a final update and break the mirror:
cluster::> snapmirror update -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_data_replica
cluster::> snapmirror break -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_data_replica

 

 

8.2 SUM DMO Command Example

 

 

# On the source application server (Linux):
$ cd /usr/sap/<SID>/SUM
$ ./STARTUP confighostagent SID=<SID> scenario=DMO targetdbtype=HDB suspendmove=true

# After initial processing, copy the SUM directory to the cloud target.
# Then on the target system:
$ cd /usr/sap/<SID>/SUM
$ ./RESUME systemmove

 

 

Note: The actual commands depend on your SUM version and system configuration.

8.3 Example of a Trigger for Near-Zero Downtime (Simplified)

 

 

CREATE TABLE myschema.LOG_TABLE_X (
    ID NUMBER,
    OPERATION VARCHAR2(10),
    CHANGED_AT DATE,
    PRIMARY KEY (ID, CHANGED_AT)
);

CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER myschema.TR_TABLE_X_AFTER
AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON myschema.TABLE_X
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    IF INSERTING THEN
        INSERT INTO myschema.LOG_TABLE_X (ID, OPERATION, CHANGED_AT)
        VALUES (:NEW.ID, 'INSERT', SYSDATE);
    ELSIF UPDATING THEN
        INSERT INTO myschema.LOG_TABLE_X (ID, OPERATION, CHANGED_AT)
        VALUES (:NEW.ID, 'UPDATE', SYSDATE);
    ELSIF DELETING THEN
        INSERT INTO myschema.LOG_TABLE_X (ID, OPERATION, CHANGED_AT)
        VALUES (:OLD.ID, 'DELETE', SYSDATE);
    END IF;
END;

 

 

This example is simplified. In a real system, SAP’s NZDT tools would manage such triggers automatically to capture changes during the conversion.

9. Things we've learned and what we're doing now to make things better

Based on my personal experience, here are some best practices to ensure a smooth migration:

- Pre-Migration Work is Critical: Archive old data, fix custom code, and prepare your cloud environment well in advance.

- Practice Makes Perfect: Multiple Dry Runs can reveal hidden bottlenecks. In one project, our second rehearsal reduced downtime by nearly 30%.

- Use Proven Tools: It has been demonstrated that a combination of SAP's SUM DMO and nZDM with NetApp SnapMirror has the potential to significantly reduce downtime. In some cases, projects have shown a reduction in final downtime from over 20 hours to just a few.

- Plan a Clear Cutover Schedule: A detailed timeline with defined go/no-go checkpoints and fallback procedures is essential.

- Continuous Monitoring: After completing the migration, keep a close watch on performance and costs at all times, drawing on SAP’s metrics and native cloud insights to spot any issues early.

-Communication and Team Preparedness: If everyone talks clearly during the cutover, there will be less confusion and any problems can be solved more quickly.

10. Conclusion and Key Takeaways

Migrating from SAP ECC 6 to S/4HANA in the cloud is challenging but achievable with careful planning and the right tools. Here are the key points to remember:

- Plan Thoroughly: Complete as much work as possible before the actual downtime. This includes system cleanup, data archiving, and multiple Dry Runs.

- Select the Right Migration Approach: No matter if you pick Greenfield, Brownfield, or Hybrid, it's important to know how each one affects downtime.

- Leverage SAP Tools: Use SUM DMO and Near-Zero Downtime Maintenance to move as many conversion tasks as possible to uptime.

- Utilize NetApp SnapMirror: Before copying your data, save it to the cloud. This'll reduce the amount of data that needs to be transferred at the end. It will also make sure you stay within the 48-hour window.

- Prepare a Detailed Cutover Plan: Define each step during the downtime, from user freeze to final validation, and always have a fallback plan.

- Monitor and Optimize: After the system is launched, keep an eye on how it's performing and how much it's costing to make sure everything is running smoothly.

In my experience, combining these techniques has enabled many organizations to achieve a seamless S/4HANA conversion with minimal downtime. I encourage you to use these best practices and tailor them to your own environment. This will allow you to move to SAP S/4HANA in the cloud with the assurance that your business operations will continue with little interruption.

Thank you for reading, and I hope this article helps you plan a successful migration. If you have any questions or need further assistance, please feel free to reach out to NetApp’s SAP Solutions Architects or your trusted partners.

entry.title: オンプレからクラウドへのSAP S/4HANAコンバージョン計画と手順 - Part 1 : 移行スケジュールとダウンタイムの最適化
entry.content:
※ 以下は私が執筆した英語版ブログの抄訳となります。原文はこちらです。

 

1. はじめに：なぜスケジュール管理とダウンタイムの最適化が重要なのか

 

SAPの移行を成功させるには、スケジュールを綿密に計画し、ダウンタイムを最小化することが非常に大切です。たとえば、財務（FI）や販売（SD）、物流（MM）などの業務プロセスは、週末48時間といった限られた時間枠での長時間停止が難しく、分刻みで慎重に進める必要があります。ポイントは、システムが稼働している間に可能な限りのタスクを済ませ、実際のダウンタイム中には最終工程だけを行うようにすることです。

本記事では、SAPの標準ツールとストレージのレプリケーション技術を組み合わせることで、ビジネスへの影響を最小限に抑えつつスムーズに移行を行う方法を解説します。

2. 移行手法の理解

2.1 グリーンフィールド、ブラウンフィールド、ハイブリッド手法

ECCからS/4HANAに移行する場合、主に3つのアプローチが考えられます。

- グリーンフィールド（新規導入）：新規にS/4HANAをクラウド上で一から構築し、必要なデータのみ移行します。まっさらな環境から始めるため、既存プロセスを再定義できる利点がありますが、多くのプロセスを作り直す必要があります。ダウンタイムは最終的なデータ移行のタイミングに集中する傾向があります。

- ブラウンフィールド（システム変換）：現行のECCシステムをS/4HANAに変換し、既存のデータやカスタマイズを引き継ぎます。ただし、何も対策をしないとダウンタイムが長くなりがちなので、SAPツールなどによる最適化が必要です。

- ハイブリッド（選択的データ移行／ブルーフィールド）：ECCシステムの一部を残しつつ、他の部分は新規に導入するアプローチです。柔軟性が高い一方で、構成が複雑になる場合があります。

2.2 アプローチ別のダウンタイムに対する影響

- グリーンフィールド：新しいシステムを並行で構築し、最終的に切り替えるため、ダウンタイムは最終的な切り替え時のみとなり、比較的に短縮することが可能です。

- ブラウンフィールド：現行システムを一括でS/4HANAに変換するため、最適化されない場合は長時間のシステムダウンにつながる可能性があります。

- ハイブリッド：選択的データ移行（一部を残し、一部を再実装）を行うため、特定のツールを使いながら、過去データや設定を取捨選択して新しいS/4HANAに移行することが多いです。

3. 移行準備

3.1 システムクリーンアップとデータアーカイブ

- データの整理：不要なデータをアーカイブまたは削除して、移行対象データ量を減らします。

- カスタムコードの見直し:ABAP Test Cockpitなどでカスタムコードを洗い出し、問題を修正します。あるプロジェクトでは、過去のデータを整理した結果、移行時間が30%短縮できた事例もあります。

3.2 クラウド環境の準備

- Infrastructure as Code (IaC)：Terraformなどのツールにより、同一の設定を用いてクラウド環境に素早く構築します。

- 事前インストール：切り替え前にS/4HANAソフトウェアをクラウド環境へ導入し、基本設定を行います。OSやネットワークの事前準備を進め、作業時の負担を軽減します。

3.3 NetApp SnapMirrorを使った事前のデータコピー

NetAppのSnapMirror機能を使えば、オンプレミスのデータを切り替え前にクラウドへ複製しておくことが可能です。

- 初期ベースライン：データベースファイルやSAP Global Directory、輸送ディレクトリなどの重要ボリュームをオンプレからクラウド（例: AWS FSx for ONTAP、Azure NetApp Files、Google Cloud NetApp Volumes）へまるごとコピーします。

- 増分更新：1時間ごと、あるいはそれ以上の頻度で差分を転送し、実際の切り替え時に送るデータを最小化します。

- 切り替え同期：移行を実施する週末に最終のSnapMirror更新を実行します。更新が完了した段階でSnapMirror関係を切断し、クラウド側のボリュームを書き込み可能な状態にします。

構成例：

# Create destination volume on the cloud NetApp system
cluster::> volume create -vserver CloudSVM -volume PRD_HANA_Data_Replica -aggregate aggr1 -size 5TB -type DP

# Establish SnapMirror relationship
cluster::> snapmirror create -source-path OnPremSVM:PRD_HANA_Data -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_Data_Replica -type XDP -schedule hourly

# Initialize SnapMirror
cluster::> snapmirror initialize -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_Data_Replica

# At cutover, run a final update and break the mirror
cluster::> snapmirror update -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_Data_Replica
cluster::> snapmirror break -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_Data_Replica

この作業を行うことにより、切り替えを実施する週末の最終データ転送は最後の増分以降に変化した部分だけに限定され、ダウンタイムを大幅に短縮できます。

4.ダウンタイム最適化のためのSAP標準ツール

4.1 Software Update Manager (SUM) と Database Migration Option (DMO)

SUM DMO は、ECCからS/4HANAへ移行する際の主要なツールです。

- 統合プロセス：アップグレードとデータベース移行をひとつのプロセスに集約することにより、全体的なダウンタイムを削減します。

- ダウンタイム最適化モード：ダウンタイム最適化モードでは、システムがまだ稼働中の段階で大部分のデータ変換を実施し、ダウンタイム中には最後の差分を適用するだけにとどめます。48時間の切り替え枠を満たしたい場合は、この方法が非常に重要になります。

コマンド例 (簡略版):

# Start SUM in downtime-optimized mode on the source system
$ cd /usr/sap/<SID>/SUM
$ ./STARTUP confighostagent SID=<SID> scenario=DMO targetdbtype=HDB suspendmove=true

その後、ターゲット・システム上での準備が完了したら、以下の処理を実行します。

cd /usr/sap/<SID>/SUM
$ ./RESUME systemmove

（プロジェクト固有のSUMバージョンに応じてコマンドは異なります。詳細は各プロジェクトのドキュメントを参照してください。）

4.2 Near-Zero Downtime Maintenance (nZDM)

Near-Zero Downtime Maintenance (nZDM) は、システム変換時にトランザクションの変更を取得、及び再適用することにより、ダウンタイムを限りなく少なくする手法です。

- データキャプチャ：データベーストリガーやレプリケーションによってシステム稼働中に変更を記録します。

- デルタの再適用：切り替え時にソース側を停止し、その最後の変更（デルタ）をS/4HANA側に短時間で適用します。

- 結果：大規模システムでも、ダウンタイムを数時間程度まで削減できる可能性が高いです。

4.3 ツールを組み合わせたダウンタイム最小化

SAP標準ツールとNetApp SnapMirrorを組み合わせることにより、以下を実現することが可能です。

- 事前にオンプレのデータをほぼすべてクラウドに転送し、切り替え時は最終差分のみを移行します。

- SUM DMOのダウンタイム最適化モードを使い、システムの稼働中に大半の変換を行います。

- ダウンタイムをさらに抑えたい場合は、nZDM（Near Zero Downtime Migration）を導入して直近の更新分をほぼリアルタイムで取り込み、適用します。

これらのプロセスを国をまたいで移行する前に行うと、実際に仕事をしない時間帯にやるべき作業量を削減することが可能です。48時間の間で対応すべきタスクは、最終的な変更と切り替え処理だけに絞れるはずです。

5. 移行を実施する週末の計画

48時間の枠を満たすには、正確なタイムラインが不可欠です。以下はサンプルの計画となります。

5.1 移行前準備 (数週間〜数日前)

- データ整理：不要データのアーカイブや削除、カスタムコード問題の解決を行います。

- クラウド環境の構築：IaC（例：Terraform）を用いてS/4HANAシステムを事前に用意します。

- データレプリケーション: NetApp SnapMirrorでオンプレのデータを事前にクラウドへコピーし、定期的に増分更新を行います。

- リハーサル：本番同様のテスト環境で最低2回はリハーサルを行い、所要時間を計測・最適化します。

5.2 切り替えの実施 (48時間内)

金曜の夕方：

- ユーザー凍結：利用者にシステム停止を告知し、すべてのトランザクションを停止します。

- 最終データ同期：SnapMirrorで最後の差分を転送します。SnapMirrorのリレーションシップを停止して、クラウド側ボリュームを書き込み可能にします。

- ECCの停止：ECCシステムを完全に停止します。

金曜の深夜：

- SUM DMOによる変換開始：ターゲットS/4HANA環境で変換プロセスを起動します。

- リアルタイムモニタリング：Basis管理者がログと性能指標を注視します。

土曜日：

- 変換の進行：SUM DMOの各フェーズ（エクスポート、インポート、変換等）が進行します。nZDMを使用する場合は直前の変更を捕捉します。

- テスト：主要トランザクションやレポートが正常動作するかの初期チェックを行います。

土曜の深夜/日曜の早朝：

- 最終デルタ適用 (nZDM利用時)：本番システムを停止し、最終的な変更分を適用します。

- スイッチオーバー：インターフェースを張り替え、DNSを更新し、業務再開の準備を行います。

- 移行後の検証：ST03N, ST06, EarlyWatch Alertなどでデータの整合性や性能を確認します。

5.3 移行後の作業

- 検証：関係者間で協力し、最重要業務が問題なく動作するかを早急に確認します。

- バックアップ＆DR：S/4HANA向けに新しいバックアップや災害対策の手順を確立します。

- サポート体制：切り替え後の最初の営業日にはサポートチームが待機し、問題が発生した場合でも迅速に対応できるようにします。

6.リスク管理とフォールバック戦略

万全を期してもトラブルは起こり得ます。そのため、強固なフォールバックプランを用意しておくことが重要です。

6.1 Go/No-Go判定

事前に決めた時間制限（例：最後の差分同期が2時間を超えたら中断するなど）を設定し、基準を超えた場合は「No-Go」を宣言し、ECCに戻す判断ができるようにします。

6.2 ロールバック計画

ECCの最新バックアップを維持し、移行中に重大な問題が発生した場合は、旧システムに戻す選択肢を確保します。SnapMirrorでオンプレのシステムをスタンバイ状態に保つ方法も考えられます。

6.3 エスカレーションとコミュニケーション

状況を定期的に共有し明確な手順を設けることにより迅速に問題を解決するとともに、全関係者への情報共有の手段を確保します。

7. 移行後の最適化と継続的な改善

新しいS/4HANAシステムが稼働した後も業務は続きます。

7.1 パフォーマンス検証

システム稼働後すぐにST03NやST06、EarlyWatch Alert、クラウド監視ツールなどを使って性能を確認し、移行前のベンチマークと比較して問題があれば迅速に対処します。

7.2 バックアップとDR

データバックアップや災害対策が重要です。クラウド中心のプランを推奨します。SnapMirrorなどで安全にデータを保持し、常にアクセス可能な状態を維持します。

7.3 FinOpsとコスト管理

リソース利用状況を可視化し、クラウドベンダーのコスト管理ツールやFinOpsの考え方を適用してリソース割り当てを調整し、無駄な支出を回避します。

8. テクニカル観点の一例/設定サンプル

下記には、キーポイントとなるプロセスを示すためのコマンド例や設定が含まれます。

8.1　NetApp SnapMirror設定例

# On the cloud NetApp cluster, create a destination volume:
cluster::> volume create -vserver CloudSVM -volume PRD_HANA_data_replica -aggregate aggr1 -size 5TB -type DP

# On the on-premises NetApp cluster, establish the SnapMirror relationship:
cluster::> snapmirror create -source-path OnPremSVM:PRD_HANA_data -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_data_replica -type XDP -schedule hourly

# Initialize the SnapMirror relationship:
cluster::> snapmirror initialize -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_data_replica

# At cutover, perform a final update and break the mirror:
cluster::> snapmirror update -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_data_replica
cluster::> snapmirror break -destination-path CloudSVM:PRD_HANA_data_replica

8.2 SUM DMOのコマンド例

# On the source application server (Linux):
$ cd /usr/sap/<SID>/SUM
$ ./STARTUP confighostagent SID=<SID> scenario=DMO targetdbtype=HDB suspendmove=true

# After initial processing, copy the SUM directory to the cloud target.
# Then on the target system:
$ cd /usr/sap/<SID>/SUM
$ ./RESUME systemmove

※ 実際のコマンドはSUMのバージョンやシステム構成によって異なります。

8.3 Near-Zero Downtime用トリガーの例（簡略版）

CREATE TABLE myschema.LOG_TABLE_X (
    ID NUMBER,
    OPERATION VARCHAR2(10),
    CHANGED_AT DATE,
    PRIMARY KEY (ID, CHANGED_AT)
);

CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER myschema.TR_TABLE_X_AFTER
AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON myschema.TABLE_X
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    IF INSERTING THEN
        INSERT INTO myschema.LOG_TABLE_X (ID, OPERATION, CHANGED_AT)
        VALUES (:NEW.ID, 'INSERT', SYSDATE);
    ELSIF UPDATING THEN
        INSERT INTO myschema.LOG_TABLE_X (ID, OPERATION, CHANGED_AT)
        VALUES (:NEW.ID, 'UPDATE', SYSDATE);
    ELSIF DELETING THEN
        INSERT INTO myschema.LOG_TABLE_X (ID, OPERATION, CHANGED_AT)
        VALUES (:OLD.ID, 'DELETE', SYSDATE);
    END IF;
END;

上記の例は簡略化しています。実際の環境では、SAPのNZDTツールが自動的にこうしたトリガーを管理し、変換中の変更をキャプチャします。

9. 教訓及び改善するために取り組むべきこと

私自身の経験をもとに、移行をスムーズに進めるためのベストプラクティスを以下に記載します。

- 事前作業が肝心：古いデータのアーカイブや不要なカスタムコードの修正、クラウド環境の準備を切り替え前に完了させることが重要です。

- リハーサルが成功のカギ：複数回のドライランを行うことにより、隠れたボトルネックを発見することが可能です。あるプロジェクトでは、2回目のリハーサルを実施することで、ダウンタイムを30%近く短縮することができました。

- 実績あるツールの活用：SAPのSUM DMOとnZDM、NetApp SnapMirrorを組み合わせることにより、ダウンタイムを大幅に減らせるケースが多く、20時間以上かかっていたダウンタイムがわずか数時間に収まった事例も存在します。

- 切り替えスケジュールは明確に：Go/No-Goの判断ポイントやフォールバック手順を細かく決めたタイムラインが必須です。

- 移行後も継続監視：移行を完了してからも常に性能やコストを注視し、SAPの指標やクラウドのネイティブ監視ツールを活用して早期に問題を発見し、対処しましょう。

- コミュニケーションとチームの準備: 切り替え時に明確な情報共有を行うことで混乱が減少し、問題が起きても速やかに解決できます。

10. まとめと主要ポイント

ECC6からS/4HANAをクラウドへ移行するのは大変ですが、綿密な計画と適切なツールを使えば実現可能です。主なポイントは以下の通りです。

- 十分な事前計画：ダウンタイム前に可能な限り作業を進めます。システムクリーンアップやデータアーカイブ、複数回のドライランを実施します。

- 最適な移行手法の選択：グリーンフィールドかブラウンフィールドかハイブリッドかにより、ダウンタイムへの影響が異なることを把握します。

- SAPのツールを活用：SUM DMOやNear-Zero Downtime Maintenanceを使い、システム稼働中に大半の変換を完了します。

- NetApp SnapMirrorを有効活用：データを事前にクラウドへコピーすることにより、最終的な転送量が削減され、48時間の制限内に収まりやすくなります。

- 綿密な切り替え計画：ユーザー凍結から最終検証までの手順を細かく設定し、フォールバックプランも準備します。

- モニタリングと最適化：本番稼働後も常に性能とコストを見守り、システムがスムーズに動くよう管理します。

私の経験上、これらの手法を組み合わせることにより、S/4HANAへの移行において最小限のダウンタイムでスムーズな切り替えを実現できます。ぜひ自社の環境に合わせてベストプラクティスを取り入れ、クラウドでのSAP S/4HANA運用へ踏み出してみてください。

最後までお読みいただき、ありがとうございます。本記事が移行計画の一助となれば幸いです。ご不明点やさらなるサポートが必要な場合は、ぜひNetAppのSAPソリューションアーキテクトや信頼できるパートナー様へご相談ください。

entry.title: SAP IBP Configuration - Create a Time Profile in SAP IBP
entry.content:
A time profile in SAP IBP defines the time dimension for planning and managing data. It consists of various levels of time buckets, such as days, weeks, months, quarters, and years. Here are some key aspects:

  • Time Buckets: These are non-overlapping periods that form the timeline, such as days, weeks, or months.
  • Hierarchy: Time profile levels form a hierarchy, where each level can have one or more parent levels. For example, days can roll up into weeks, which then roll up into months.
  • Customization: Time profiles can be customized to fit specific business needs, allowing for flexible organization of planning data.
  • Aggregation and Disaggregation: Time profiles support the aggregation and disaggregation of data across different time levels, enabling detailed and summary views.

Creating a time profile involves defining these time buckets and their relationships, ensuring continuous and non-overlapping periods.

1. On the SAP Fiori launchpad home page, choose the Time Profiles tile.

2. Choose New and select the time profile levels shown in the Levels Screen Area table.

 

3. Enter ID, name, description, start date, and end date.

4. Enter the values for the default display horizons.

Default Display Horizon in the Past

This setting determines the period that is preselected for the relevant time profile level when you create a new planning view or edit an existing planning view in the SAP Integrated Business Planning, add-in for Microsoft Excel. The value you specify here is relative to the current period and the corresponding time period is displayed by default in the From field of the Time Settings tab on the Create New Planning View and the Edit Planning View screens of the add-in.

Default Display Horizon in the Future

This setting determines the period that is preselected for the relevant time profile level when you create a new planning view or edit an existing planning view in the SAP Integrated Business Planning, add-in for Microsoft Excel. The value you specify here is relative to the current period and the corresponding time period is displayed by default in the To field of the Time Settings tab on the Create New Planning View and the Edit Planning View screens of the add-in.

entry.title: From Syntax to Semantics: A Classification of Core Data Services (CDS)
entry.content:
Table of Contents

  1. Introduction
  2. Topology Layers
  3. Logical Layer
  4. CDS Object Tree
  5. CDS and SQL
  6. CDS System Function
  7. CDS Scalar Function and CDS Table Function
  8. CDS System Entities
  9. Linguo-Technical Layer
  10. Mapping CDS Language to CDS Source Code and CDS Object
  11. Syntactic Layer
  12. CDS by Keyword
  13. Keyword Semantic
  14. Business Layer
  15. VDM Type
  16. CDS Purpose
  17. CDS by Stability Contract
  18. CDS by Lifecycle
  19. CDS by Modelling Pattern

1 Introduction

Recently, I read the ABAP keyword documentation to get an overview of the CDS framework from a high-level perspective. I came across numerous terms, each defined in the keyword dictionary. For example, terms like CDS Objects, DDL, and CDS Entity were mentioned, among others.

This prompted me to organize and systematize these terms to better understand their roles and why the documentation authors chose to highlight them.

As a result, I came up with the idea of creating a CDS classification. Of course, this topology is not exhaustive.

This article turned out to be quite a long read, and I hope, dear reader, that you won’t mind. Maybe you’ll find a moment to go through this classification while sipping your latte on a winter evening. Otherwise, feel free to use the table of contents above to navigate quickly through the article.

I’ve only gathered high-level information here, so I encourage you to dive deeper if you're interested.

If you prefer the article is available on my GitHub: https://github.com/IlyaPrusakou/CDSClassification.git

2 Topology Layers

The CDS typology begins by dividing the CDS framework into four layers:

1 Logical
2 Linguo-technical
3 Syntactic
4 Business

The first three layers are derived from the keyword documentation, while the fourth—Business—is based on my experience and active googling.

3 Logical Layer

At the foundation of the Logical level is the term CDS object. I would define a CDS object as a programmatic entity that encapsulates all runtime properties of a CDS view. We refer to a CDS as an object when it is used as a type in ABAP code, as part of an Open SQL selection, or in similar contexts.

4 CDS Object Tree

I would represent CDS objects as the following tree:

1 CDS objects

1.1 CDS annotations

1.2 CDS metadata extensions

1.3 CDS entities
1.3.1 CDS view entities
1.3.2 CDS projections view
1.3.2.1 CDS Transactional query
1.3.2.2 CDS Analytical projection view
1.3.2.3 CDS Transactional interface
1.3.3 CDS Table functions
1.3.4 CDS Hierarchies
1.3.5 CDS Custom Entities
1.3.6 CDS Abstract Entities
1.3.7 CDS DDIC-based views
1.3.8 CDS External Entities
1.3.9 ABAP CDS Aspect

1.4 CDS Tunning Objects
1.4.1 CDS Entity Buffers

1.5 CDS User Defined Types
1.5.1 CDS Simple Types
1.5.2 CDS Enumeration Types

1.6 CDS Functions
1.6.1 CDS Scalar Functions
1.6.1.1 CDS SQL-based Scalar Functions
1.6.1.2 CDS Analytical Scalar Functions
1.6.2 CDS Built-in Functions

1.7 CDS Roles

5 CDS and SQL

Also, it's worth to mention It is also worth mentioning the classification of CDS Entities into two categories:

  1. CDS SQL Entities – This includes all entities except CDS non-SQL entities.

  2. CDS Non-SQL Entities – These are specialized entities such as Abstract Entities, Custom Entities, and CDS Analytical Projection Views.

6 CDS System Function

Another important aspect to clarify is the semantic overlap between CDS user-defined functions and CDS system functions. The overarching term for both is CDS Scalar Function, which can be further classified as follows:

  1. CDS Scalar Function
    1.1 Analytical Scalar Function - Classified as a System Function
    1.2 SQL-Based Scalar Function
        1.2.1 Delivered by SAP - Classified as a System Function
        1.2.2 User-Defined Scalar Function

7 CDS Scalar Function and CDS Table Function

I would also like to emphasize the dichotomy between CDS Table Functions and CDS Scalar Functions. While CDS Table Functions are classified as a type of CDS Entity, CDS Scalar Functions fall under the category of CDS Functions.

8 CDS System Entities

Lastly, it’s worth noting that SAP provides special CDS System Entities, which are predefined entities. However, for the sake of clarity and consistency, I propose classifying them under CDS Entities, specifically as entities delivered by SAP.

9 Linguo-Technical Layer

At the core of the Technical level lies the term CDS source code. Why is this significant? The answer is that CDS source code is used by developers to define all the properties of a CDS object. Additionally, it serves for transporting CDS objects between systems and generating the corresponding runtime artifacts.

10 Mapping CDS Language to CDS Source Code and CDS Object

In summary, I propose distinguishing CDS objects as runtime artifacts and CDS source code as design-time artifacts. The classification can be outlined as follows:

 

Source Code TypeSource Code SubtypeCDS Object
CDS Source CodeCDS Source CodeCDS Object
Data Definition Language (DDL) CDS Entity (e.g., CDS View Entity, CDS Custom Entity, etc.)
 DDL Annotation (DDLA)CDS Annotation
 DDL Metadata Extension (DDLX)CDS Metadata Extension
Data Control Language (DCL) CDS Roles
Type Definition Language (TDL) CDS User-Defined Types
Function Definition Language (FDL) CDS Function

I deliberately exclude Service Definition Language (SDL), as it pertains to a different topic—the RAP framework. Nevertheless, it’s worth noting the close relationship between Service Definitions and the CDS framework.

Additionally, it’s important to highlight that the languages used for annotations and metadata extensions (DDLA and DDLX) are sub-dialects of the broader DDL.

11 Syntactic Layer

In the Syntactic Layer, I am going to propose a classification of CDS entities based on their primary defining keyword. This approach organizes CDS artifacts according to the central syntax element that dictates their structure and functionality. By focusing on the main keyword, we can establish a clear and systematic understanding of the various CDS types.

12 CDS by Keyword

1. Syntax of ABAP CDS

1.1 DEFINE ANNOTATION
1.2 ANNOTATE VIEW
1.3 ANNOTATE ENTITY

1.4 DEFINE VIEW ENTITY

1.5 DEFINE VIEW ENTITY AS PROJECTION ON
Provide Contract: Transactional Query

1.6 DEFINE VIEW ENTITY AS PROJECTION ON
Provide Contract: Transactional Interface

1.7 DEFINE TRANSIENT VIEW ENTITY AS PROJECTION ON
Provide Contract: Analytical Query

1.8 DEFINE VIEW
1.9 DEFINE TABLE FUNCTION
1.10 DEFINE HIERARCHY
1.11 DEFINE CUSTOM ENTITY
1.12 DEFINE ABSTRACT ENTITY
1.13 DEFINE EXTERNAL ENTITY
1.14 DEFINE VIEW ENTITY BUFFER

1.15 DEFINE ROLE
1.16 DEFINE ACCESS POLICY

1.17 DEFINE TYPE
1.18 DEFINE TYPE ENUM

1.19 DEFINE SCALAR FUNCTION

1.20 DEFINE ASPECT

2 Extension Syntax

2.1 EXTEND VIEW ENTITY
2.2 EXTEND CUSTOM ENTITY
2.3 EXTEND ABSTRACT ENTITY
2.4 EXTEND VIEW

13 Keyword Semantic

To express the semantics of the CDS main keyword, I have adopted a notation similar to Backus-Naur Form (BNF), which is commonly used to describe the syntax of programming languages. In this approach, the CDS main keyword is constructed using the following components:

 

<keyword> ::= <cds_action> <cds_type>
<cds_action> ::= define | extend | annotate

<cds_type> ::= view | <entity> | <view_entity> | role | table_function | aspect | hierarchy | <type> | scalar_function | view_entity_buffer

<entity> ::= [custom | abstract | external] entity

<view_entity>  ::= [transient] view entity [as projection] [<contract>]

<contract> ::= provide contract transactional {query | interface} | analytical query

<type> ::= type | type enum

 

14 Business Layer

The Business Layer represents the practical application of CDS as a modeling pillar, enabling the creation of business entities based on the technical foundation provided by the CDS framework. One of the key guides within this layer is the Virtual Data Model (VDM), which standardizes the design and structure of CDS-based entities to align with business requirements.

VDM acts like a blueprint, similar to how Lego bricks can be assembled to build a castle. In this analogy, the Lego bricks represent the CDS framework components, while the final model—be it a Remote API, Value Help, or another business construct—symbolizes the resulting business entity. This approach ensures consistency, reusability, and alignment with SAP's overall architecture principles.

15 VDM Type

The division of the Business Layer relies on the values of the VDM.viewType annotation. This annotation is a key component used by SAP for the internal structuring and interpretation of CDS views. It defines the purpose and role of each CDS view within the overall VDM architecture. Below are the specific types of VDM views:

1 Basic CDS View

Represents the foundational layer of the VDM. Typically maps to a single database table to provide raw data. Used as a building block for higher-level CDS views.

2 Composite CDS View

Combines multiple Basic CDS Views or other Composite Views. Provides aggregated, enriched, or contextualized data. Acts as a middle layer in the VDM hierarchy.

3 Consumption CDS View

Designed for use in user interfaces, analytics, or APIs. Focuses on data presentation and consumption by external applications. Often leverages SAP Fiori or analytical tools.

4 Extension CDS View

Used to extend existing CDS views without modifying the original definition. Enables custom fields or logic to be added to standard SAP-delivered views.

5 Derivation Function

Facilitates the calculation of derived fields or values within a CDS view. They are often used for derivation values for CDS parameters.

6 Transactional

Supports transactional use cases. Often used for modelling business objects(e.g. RAP objects) and associated with data modification or interactions in SAP’s transactional scenarios.

16 CDS Purpose

CDS Purpose Classification:

1 Views Providing Data Access

These views interact with the underlying database and are used for various data access purposes:

1.1 Interface CDS View

Acts as an interface for data reading, often used as a source in Open SQL or as select source in other CDS views

1.2 Restricted Reuse CDS View

Provides entities with specific restrictions for usage outside of particular application area. Restricted nature means that the CDS should not be used by customers or SAP partners in their developments. Don't confuse with Private CDS.

1.3 Consumption CDS View

Focused on presenting data for analytics or user interfaces (e.g., SAP Fiori). Therefore they contain a plenty of consumption specific metadata.

1.4 Private CDS View

Intended for internal use as constituting part of other non-private CDS. Once it is created, it is used only in one specific place in CDS hierarchy.

1.5 Transactional Processing CDS View

They are used for modelling CDS tree of business objects and enable capability of adding transactional behavior to them.

1.6 Remote API CDS View

Exposes data as part of remote APIs for integration with external consumers. While Consumption CDS are created for specific consumption case providing a set consumption specific metadata, Remote API CDS are consumption and client agnostic reducing any redundant metadata.

1.7 View Extends

Extends an existing CDS view to add additional fields or logic while keeping the original definition intact.

1.8 View Extension Include

Created by key user extensibility supplier to enable this extension option for customers. To better understand this topic, I recommend exploring the Key User Extensibility documentation. Essentially, these views serve as anchors for further extensions views generated via key user apps

1.9 Derivation Function

Performs derived field calculations within the CDS view.

2 Views Without Data Access

These views do not directly access data but serve other modeling purposes:

2.1 Abstract View

Provides an abstract structure used for ABAP typing and RAP action parameter modelling.

17 CDS by Stability Contract

By stability contract:

1 C0 Extend
These entities can be extended, with specific usage contexts:

1.1 Use in Key User Apps
Extension point is designed for extensions via key user tools.

1.2 Use in Cloud Development
Extension point is suitable for extensions in cloud-based development scenarios(in ADT tool).

1.3 Both context
Can be used in both key user apps and cloud development environments.

2 C1 Use System-Internally
These entities are intended for internal system usage:

2.1 Use in Key User Apps
View is visible in objects with language version ABAP for Key Users.

2.2 Use in Cloud Development
View is visible in objects with language version ABAP for Cloud Development.

3 C2 Use as Remote API
These entities are exposed as remote APIs for external consumption.

18 CDS by Lifecycle

By lifecycle status:

1 Released
CDS views that are officially released for productive use. SAP and partner and customers shall utilize them in full grade.

2 Deprecated
CDS views that are marked for future removal. While still available, successor view is provided to substitute of deprecated one. Their usage is discouraged and all interesting actors have to adopt their code to replace with successor view.

3 Decommissioned
CDS views that are no longer available or supported. The code adoption is completed. A particular time, usually defined by product owner, for notification of customers is over. They can be removed from the system at any time.

19 CDS by Modelling Pattern

1 None
Basic CDS views without any specific modeling pattern applied.

2 Auxiliary Views
These views serve auxiliary purposes, such as supporting additional features or providing helper structures:

2.1 Data Structure: These entities are primarily used for parameter modeling in RAP actions or functions and for defining structured variable types in ABAP code. Typically, such CDS entities are not combined with other supported capabilities. Their exlusive purpose is to serve as data structures

2.2 Language Dependent:These CDS entities provide translations or language-dependent texts. They are often based on text tables or table with domain values. Typically, they are linked to text consumer entities through associations.

2.3 Value Help Provider: These CDS entities are used to supply value help for fields, such as dropdown lists or input assistance. They are typically based on database tables or existing CDS entities that contain the relevant value set. Value help providers are linked to consumer entities through associations and annotations, ensuring seamless integration with user interfaces.

2.4 Collective Value Help: It refers to a CDS entity that combines multiple value help sources into a single, unified value help. This is useful when a field requires input assistance from different datasets or when the value help is context-specific.

2.5 Derivation Function: It is a mechanism in SAP CDS views used to calculate or derive values dynamically based on specific business logic or conditions. These functions are often used to populate fields, restrict data, or enhance query results by deriving context-specific values, such as defaulting fiscal periods, calculating derived attributes, or mapping inputs to outputs. The CDS entity can be used as Derivation Function in an Consumption.derivation annotation.

6 Parent-Child Hierarchy Node Provider
Used for hierarchical data structures, enabling navigation between parent and child nodes. The CDS entity defines a parent-child hierarchy. Requests from the entity return hierarchy nodes with additional hierarchy-specific information, for example the size of the subhierarchy starting at a node. The entity can be used in hierarchy-navigation and hierarchy-aggregation functions.

7 Enterprise Search Provider
These view entities are annotated with framework-specific annotations EnterpriseSearch. The annotations define search capabilities and they also trigger the generation of a CDS-based search connector. CDS-based enterprise search provides enhanced search capabilities in SAP Fiori launchpad.

8 RAP Framework
Focused on the SAP RESTful Application Programming model:

8.1 Transactional Interface: A CDS transactional interface is a CDS projection view that is intended to serve as stable public interface for consumption. A CDS transactional interface should be classified by a release contract(don't mixed up with provider contract) and thus serve as released API. They are typically used in the context of the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model to provide the basis for a RAP BO interface. Technically, BO interface layer consists of a CDS projection view with the provider contract transactional_interface and a Behavior Definition Interface with implementation type interface. A CDS transactional interface is defined using the CDS DDL statement DEFINE VIEW ENTITY AS PROJECTION ON and it must have its provider contract set to TRANSACTIONAL_INTERFACE. A CDS transactional interface is a CDS persistent entity.

8.2 Transactional Query: A CDS projection view that is intended for modeling transactional queries. A transactional query is typically used in the context of the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model to adapt a CDS data model for service-specific use cases (They are substitution of Consumption views). A transactional query is defined using the CDS DDL statement DEFINE VIEW ENTITY AS PROJECTION ON and it must have the provider contract set to TRANSACTIONAL_QUERY. A CDS transactional query is a CDS persistent entity.

9 Analytical Framework
Designed for analytics and reporting:

9.1 Analytical Query: Analytical queries are views that selects data from your analytical data model, based on specific use-cases requirements. Queries can also define the initial layout that is displayed in the output, and can be used to perform various analytical evaluations and calculations. They expose defined measurement and dimensions

9.2 Analytical Cube: Data cube represents the multidimensional data model. All fields(except key fields) should be defined as dimensions or measures. The cube view is the center of a Star/Snowflake schema referencing dimension views (annotated with Analytics.dataCategory: #DIMENSION). CDS view as cube view has annotation Analytics.dataCategory: #CUBE in the header. A cube is the data source of an analytical query (analytical projection) and must contain at least one measure.

9.3 Analytical Dimension: A dimension view provides extended information for combination of dimension and measure fields in Cube like attributes, texts, hierarchies. The view is annotated with Analytics.dataCategory: #DIMENSION

9.4 Analytical Parent-Child Hierarchy Node: The CDS entity can be used as a parent-child hierarchy in analytics. It is either an analytical dimension, or connected to an analytical dimension. The rows of the entity represent nodes of the hierarchy. Currently only hierarchies defined by annotation Hierarchy.parentChild can be used in analytic models.

9.5 Analytical Document Store: These CDS entities are usually annotated with: ObjectModel.supportedCapabilities: #ANALYTICAL_DOCUMENT_STORE and ObjectModel.modellingPattern: #ANALYTICAL_DOCUMENT_STORE. Also, they have a specific data category: Analytics.dataCategory: #DOCSTORE. Moreover, these CDS entities should be linked with the parent CDS Analytics Cube via the annotation Analytics.document.storageForEntity: ['Name_of_Cube']. And last but not least, the annotation Analytics.document.serviceClassName: '<IF_RSDOC_SERVICE_VIR>' should be placed in the CDS header. A CDS-based Document Store handles Document IDs and their assignment to cells in a query result. The actual documents are handled by the application. To achieve this, the application must implement a class that implements a special interface IF_RSDOC_SERVICE_VIRT, and the name of this class must be assigned to the Document CDS view.

9.6 Analytical Fact: Facts are an optional part of an analytical data model in ABAP analytics. A fact can be used as a data source for a cube. The fact view has annotation Analytics.dataCategory: #FACT in the header

9.7 Analytical KPI:The Analytical KPI CDS is designed to represent Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) within SAP's analytical frameworks. KPIs provide measurable values that help organizations assess the performance of their processes, operations, or objectives.

10 Output Management
Enables data preparation for outputs like forms and emails:

10.1 Output Form Data Provider: Supplies data for forms.

10.2 Output Email Data Provider: Provides data for email templates.

10.3 Output Parameter Determination Data Source: Helps in determining parameters for output processes.

11 Situation Framework
Handles contextual situations and notifications:

11.1 Situation Anchor: Defines the primary anchor business object for a situation.

11.2 Situation Trigger: Specifies the events or triggers for a situation.

11.3 Situation Data Context: Provides the data context for evaluating situations.

12 External Data Provider
Integrates data from external sources into the CDS framework. I suppose that pattern is imposed exactly for CDS External Entities.

It's worth mentioning that for hierarchy modeling, we can also distinguish patterns such as hierarchy source CDS and CDS hierarchy directory. Moreover, CDS hierarchies can be modeled in a time-dependent or permanent manner.

Regarding points 10–11, they remain unfamiliar territory to me. Therefore, I would greatly appreciate it if you, dear reader, could share a detailed guide on them. Thank you in advance!

entry.title: End to End Designing the Multilevel Hierarchy in Datasphere
entry.content:
In this blog we would like to cover up how to design multilevel Hierarchy in Datasphere on CDS views.

I have considered Profit center in my scenario and Below are the list of standard CDS views required to design Hierarchy.

CDS View Name 

SQL VIEW NAME 

CDC 

Support DataFlow 

Support Replication flow 

I_PROFITCENTERTEXT 

IFIPROFITCENTERT 

NO 

Yes 

 

I_PROFITCENTER 

IFIPROFITCENTER 

YES 

 

Yes 

I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHY 

IFIPROFITCENTERH 

 

Yes 

 

I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHYTEXT 

 

IFIPRFTCTRHT 

 

Yes 

 

I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHYNODE 

 

IPRFTCTRHNODE 

 

Yes 

No 

I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHYNODET 

 

IFIPRFTCTRHNODET 

No 

Yes 

No 

To Achieve a hierarchy Representative key plays a key role. If we miss deciding the representative key then the Hierarchy will not be visible.

We don't have option like in BW - Tcode RSH1 in datasphere to check whether Hierarchy is enabled or not at Master data dimension level or Fact association level

What is representative key ?

In an analytical scenario where data is exposed as dimension, the master data can have multiple key fields, but a single key is required to access the data. That's why this field must be identified as a representative key field 

Here i try to explain on the standard CDS view name for better understanding while designing replace the standard view name with your custom view name in Datasphere

I_Profitcentertext : 

Semantics Usage as Text  

Preparing the Hierarchy Directory : 

I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHYTEXT ;

I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHY : This CDS view will help us to choose multiple hierarchy versions

Semantics Usage Type : Dimension

Add I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHYTEXT  as Text Association

Now moving to Preparing Hierarchy node 

I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHYNODETEXT :

I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHYNODE :

Add the below association Before choosing the semantics to  Hierarchy with Directory

I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHY

I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHYTEXT

Choose the semantics usage as Hierarchy with Directory 

Once click on the Hierarchy with Directly Setting we have to pass the fields 

Parent : once click on the field you will be able to see the dropdown and choose Parent node 

Child : Hierarchy Node

Hierarchy Directory : I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHY( for your ease of understanding i have given standard CDS view name. Here you will see the Business name of the view as per your design

Node Type : we will see 3 unique values under Node type R- ROOT , N-Node , L - Leaves

Node type values : initially it will be blank click on the + icon Next to Node type values to Add values as shown in my screenshot 

 

Now click on this icon to assign the representative key 

Add Hierarchy Node as Representative Key to this view 

 

Now the final step to add this hierarchy view to Profit center Dimension 

I_PROFITCENTER

Add I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHYNODE as association to I_PROFITCENTER

Add I_PROFITCENTERTEXT as association to I_PROFITCENTER( We can ignore this step as you can fine the Description field is available in I_PROFITCENTER itself you can decide the semantics as Text and assign the text to Profit center

Now add  I_PROFITCENTER(Dimension) as association to your fact view & Deploy .

Go to your Analytical view Click on Fact source 

In Details tab find for profit center and click on the right side arrow 

Enable the check box Use Associated Dimension to see the hierarchy 

Now click on preview once deployed.

You will see the Hiearchy option if all went well 

 

Select your Hierarchy from Drop down. 

At this moment we don't have option to apply Hierarchy Variable at analytical model level tried applying input parameter at Hierarchy dimension level which ended up with error message

Review the following errors: " - CSN Compilation failed with following messages: Errors(ORIGINAL): (in entity:“IT.2VD_RTR_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHY_01”) Table-like entities with parameters are not supported for conversion to SQL [Ln 3459] Errors(FORMATTED): (in entity:“IT.2VD_RTR_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHY_01”) Table-like entities with parameters are not supported for conversion to SQL [Ln 3459], Correlation ID: 4c684267-8ea9-42d6-44fd-3adf8d7587b3 

and SAP is releasing it in their upcoming release till the time we have to filter the required Hierarchy at I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHY view.

Here is the final Output at Analytical Model level

Are you still not able to see the Hierarchy ?? Execute the below steps 

Step 1 : Execute tcode KCH2 to display Profit center hierarchy find your hierarchy 

Step 2 : Execute Tcode HRY_REPRELEV  

SETCLASSS = 0106 (in my example)

Execute 

Step 3:  Enable the check boxes as   

  • Report Relevant  
  • Auto Replicate 

Step 4 : Click on Save 

Step 5 : No Entries before executing the Hierarchy Replication Tcode  

Step 6: Execute Tcode : HRRP_REP and select your Hiearchy name at Hierarchy ID

Step 6 : Refresh the CDS View I_PROFITCENTERHIERARCHY

entry.title: SAP RISE - Key Learnings
entry.content:
This Blog is based on my personal experience being involved in a SAP RISE migration project for a customer in UK. 

What is RISE with SAP?

  • Cloud-based ERP system SAP S/4HANA in public or private cloud
  • Cloud-based services and technical support provided by SAP
  • SAP assumes responsibility for system provisioning, hosting in the cloud, and maintenance
  • Makes use of SAP BTP for collaboration with business partners across interface 

Pre-requisites for RISE with SAP – The following points should minimize any delays with any RISE Migration project. 

  • You should have a customer specific S-User ID and have authorizations to download S/W from Market Place and are able to raise SR’s, cases and incidents.
  • Make sure the file systems on the Migration Server are sized correctly
  • Ensure that the Network, firewall changes, ports and authorizations required to access to Migration Server are in place.
  • Check your access to the SAP Share point site where all the passwords will be shared in a secure area.
  • Confirm SAP have created the Application and the DB SID and they do match
  • Ensure that the required file system storage space (SUM, additional Backup’s, Export/import)  is calculated correctly prior to the start of the migration/conversion.
  • Confirm correct Database sizing has been provided to SAP
  • Confirm SAP have created the Application and the DB host that match the same time zone
  • Add-On Compatibility – Align with 3rd Party add-on compatibility and remove obsolete Add-on’s before starting the migration
  • Use the latest SUM tool and check for issues that might affect the migration timelines
  • RISE project is delivered via ticketing system via the SAP support portal. Make sure the shared responsibility model between SAP, Migration Partner and the Customer  are agreed prior to project kick-off.

Summary

  • The above pre-requisites were noted for the SAP RISE migration scenario using System copy and S4 upgrade options.

entry.title: Re: How to terminate transaction causing high CPU Utiliza...
entry.content:
Hi Shruti,

If execution consumes high CPU (e.g. for a short or long period) not definitely means a problem. Also we need to define what is an acceptable time when execution should finish (how long should it run normally). This can be different in every execution.

So the logic is different, there is no point in auto interrupting the execution due to "high" CPU usage.

In SAP HANA database, you can do preventive actions with workload classes, so you can limit the resource which available for the specific workload class:

  • 2222250 - FAQ: SAP HANA Workload Management

Regards,
Laszlo

entry.title: A closer look at Event based Production accounting in UPA
entry.content:
UPA and the change in Production accounting from month end to Realtime 

Unless you have been living under a rock you must have already heard of the new Universal Parallel accounting that SAP has already released and is generally available for customers.

I have been working in the SAP Controlling area since the last two decades. The onset of S/4 HANA brought in some changes relating to cost elements, allocations Actual costing and material ledger. However, the production and costing side remained unchanged since times immemorial. I haven’t seen a change in the period end closing process (WIP, Variance and Settlement) in the last decade.

This is the most significant change to this process since a long time.

 

What’s changed:

I am not going to go over all that’s changed in UPA in this post, if you are not familiar with UPA you need to first start with the excellent blog posts by Janet and Sarah on this topic

UPA Overview by Sarah

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/universal-parallel-accounting-in-sap-s-4hana/ba-p/13551060

 

Inventory accounting in UPA by Janet

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/inventory-accounting-with-universal-parallel-accounting-in-sap-s-4hana-2022/ba-p/13529230

 

Event-based production accounting in SAP S/4HANA is a significant change from the traditional period-based approach, with cost postings now linked to business events rather than period-end processes. This provides real-time insights and simplifies period-end closing.

Here are the key changes:

  • Real-time insights: Cost information is up to date, allowing comparison of planned versus actual costs and variance splits.
  • Simplified period-end: Traditional period-end activities are removed.
  • Parallel accounting: Enables valuation for different accounting standards, using multiple ledgers and currencies.
  • Posting: Journal entries are created with each business transaction, replacing legacy tables.
  • WIP and Variance Calculation: Calculated immediately upon business events, rather than at period-end.
  • Reporting: New Fiori apps offer better cost and variance reviews.
  • Costing: Event-based costing sheets are necessary.
  • Results analysis key: The RSEBW key is needed for event-based posting.
  • Overhead: Material and production overheads are charged immediately.
  • Transition: Can be implemented by switching order type assignments, however period end steps may still be needed temporarily for both event-based and period-based processes.
  • Settlement: Event-based uses a posting rule, instead of the traditional settlement rule.

The scope item for event-based posting is 3F0, while the traditional approach uses BEI. Event-based is the default in SAP S/4HANA Cloud and is required for universal parallel accounting.

 

 

What’s Changed in Configuration/ Setup?

To configure event-based production accounting, several key settings need to be established within SAP S/4HANA, primarily focusing on the 3F0 scope item. These configurations ensure that costs are posted correctly based on business events rather than at period end.

 

The new IMG Node for EBPA

 

Here are the essential configuration steps,

  • Event-Based Processing Keys: Verify that the event-based processing keys, RSEBW for manufacturing orders and PCCEB for product cost collectors (or ORDMS and PCCMS for manual settlement), are available within the Event-Based WIP and Variance Posting configuration activity. These keys dictate the WIP calculation method, variance split, and valid posting receivers.

Most of the variance and WIP calculation settings are now event based and moved to one single configuration node.

  • WIP Source Accounts: Assign WIP source accounts to specify which costs should be posted and capitalized as WIP and define event-based WIP account determination rules to specify the G/L accounts to which WIP or unrealized costs are to be posted. All WIP source accounts from the Define Assignments configuration activity must be included here.
  • Event-Based Costing Sheets: Create or use a pre-delivered event-based costing sheet in the Define Costing Sheets configuration activity. Ensure the "Event-Based" checkbox is selected for the costing sheet.

 

  • Valuation Variants: Assign the event-based costing sheet to the relevant valuation variants per plant in the Define Valuation Variants per Plant configuration activity. This is crucial for overhead calculations.
    • The valuation variant must have the event-based costing sheet assigned. You can’t assign a non-event-based costing sheet.
  • Order-Type-Dependent Parameters: Assign the event-based processing key (RSEBW or ORDMS) and a planned costing variant to the relevant order types (e.g. YBM1) in the Define Order-Type-Dependent Parameters for Production Orders and Define Order-Type-Dependent Parameters for Process Orders configuration activities. For product cost collectors, assign the event-based processing key PCCEB or PCCMS in Define Cost-Accounting-Relevant Default Values for Order Types and Plants. If using RSEBW or PCCEB, the assigned planned costing variant must have an event-based costing sheet assigned to it.

            

 

  • Account Determination: Ensure that the accounts for posting variances are set up correctly under the Sourcing and Procurement area, within the Automatic Account Determination configuration activity. This includes accounts for price differences (PRD), inventory posting (BSX) and offsetting accounts for inventory posting (GBB).
  • Results Analysis Key: When creating order master data, the RA Key (results analysis key) field must be set to RSEBW for event-based production cost posting. The Event-Based Posting checkmark will then be derived automatically.
  • Settlement: With the event-based approach, settlement profiles are no longer needed. The settlement type is determined by the event-based processing key. If using RSEBW or PCCEB, settlement is triggered by business events, while ORDMS or PCCMS require manual settlement.

By completing these configurations, the system is set up to automatically post WIP, overhead, and variance in real-time, based on business events rather than waiting for period-end activities.

Event based production Postings during the month

The below table shows how WIP and variances are calculated at various stages of using Event based production accounting.

 

Original Order Status

Action to the Order

New Order Status

Event-Based Posting Results

REL (Released) or PDLV (Partially delivered)

Goods issue or activity confirmation

REL (Released) or PDLV (Partially delivered)

WIP increases.

REL (Released)

Partial goods receipt

PDLV (Partially delivered)

WIP decreases.

REL (Released) or PDLV (Partially delivered)

Final goods receipt

DLV (Delivered)

WIP is cleared and variance is settled.

Manually set Complete Technically

TECO (Technically completed)

Manually select Delivery Completed

DLV (Delivered)

TECO (Technically completed) or DLV (Delivered)

Goods issue or activity confirmation

TECO (Technically completed) or DLV (Delivered)

Variance is recalculated and resettled.

TECO (Technically completed)

Manually revoke technical completion

REL (Released)

Variance is reversed and WIP is recalculated and reposted.

DLV (Delivered)

Cancel final goods receipt

PDLV (Partially delivered)

Manually unselect Delivery Completed

 

Now, I am going to create a production order and post a goods issue to show the calculation during the month. We are going to track the progress using the apps below

  • Display Line Items - Production Accounting (F7288)
  • Work in Process - Event-Based (F3498)
  • Analyze Production Costs - Event-Based (F4059)
  • Product Cost Collector Details - Event-Based (F6965)
  • Analyze Costs by Work Center/Operation (F3331)
  • Production Cost Overview - Event-Based (F6248)
  • Display Posting Rules - Event-Based (F5228)

Step 1: Create a new Production order

Event based production costs at order creation using the app “Analyze Production costs, Event Based”

At creation, no actual costs posted yet

Step 2: Post Goods receipt and Confirmation for the Production order with minor variances in quantity

WIP Is calculated and posted right at the point of goods Issue

FI Document for WIP Posting

 

The above screenshot clearly demonstrates advantages UPA and EBPA

  • Its shown that WIP gets posted during the month in Realtime across all ledgers
  • Every ledger has a separate WIP calculation as the cost estimates by ledger are different. i.e. 0L is Legal, 2L is IFRS, 4G is group valuation.
  • WIP is calculated differently for each ledger and valuation.
  • The value is different per each ledger because each ledger has a different costing variant, valuation variant, Overhead costing sheet, Different activity prices, and material prices.
  • You can also see that 2L ledger is having a different fiscal year variant (V3) instead of K4. i.e for the first time ever in SAP we can have more than one fiscal year variant in controlling. This is only possible with UPA.
  • While 0L and 4G post in 2025, 2L posts in 11/2024 (V3 fiscal year variant)

 

How to manage errors during month end

Since we are moving from a periodic posting of WIP and variances to real-time, we need to think closely about error handling.  The below apps allow for monitoring and handling errors during the month.

 

  • Postprocess Event-Based Postings - Overhead Calculation (F5763)
  • Order Exception Monitor - Event-Based (F5133)
  • Manage Product Costing Errors (F5132)
  • Postprocess Event-Based Postings - Product Costing (F3669)
  • Schedule Product Costing Jobs (F3683)
    • Postprocess Event-Based Postings - Product Costing (Job Template)
    • Postprocess Event-Based Postings - Overhead Calculation
    • WIP Quantity Calculation for Actual Costing: Event-Based Order and PCC (only if WIP revaluation is needed in Actual Costing (33Q))

 

Errors can be monitored using the above applications.

Conclusion

Event-Based Production Accounting (EBPA) in SAP S/4HANA represents a significant paradigm shift in how companies manage their production accounting processes. This new approach offers several key advantages:

  1. Real-time insights: By posting costs as they occur, EBPA provides up-to-date financial information, enabling more timely decision-making.
  2. Simplified period-end closing: The elimination of traditional period-end activities streamlines the closing process, potentially reducing workload and improving efficiency.
  3. Enhanced parallel accounting: EBPA supports multiple ledgers and currencies, facilitating compliance with various accounting standards simultaneously.
  4. Improved cost visibility: New Fiori apps offer better tools for reviewing costs and variances, enhancing cost control and analysis capabilities.
  5. Automated overhead allocation: Material and production overheads are charged immediately, providing a more accurate picture of costs throughout the production process.

However, implementing EBPA requires careful configuration and a shift in mindset from periodic to event-driven accounting. Companies must ensure proper setup of event-based processing keys, WIP source accounts, costing sheets, and valuation variants. Additionally, they need to establish robust error handling processes to manage issues that may arise during real-time postings.

 

While the transition to EBPA may present initial challenges, the long-term benefits of more accurate, timely, and flexible production accounting make it a valuable evolution for organizations using SAP S/4HANA. As companies continue to adapt to this new approach, they are likely to discover further opportunities for optimization and improved financial management in their manufacturing processes.

 

Q&A


Feel free to ask any questions or share your experience in the comments section below. I will try to answer questions as soon as I can. Alternatively, you can reach out to me on my LinkedIn page below. Follow me there for further updates,

https://ca.linkedin.com/in/naveen-pidikiti

entry.title: Re: ABAP CDS Cheat Sheet: Amounts and Quantities in ABAP CDS
entry.content:
Hi Andrea - I have a requirement to get duplicate entries based on a CDS view but the duplicates are based on 2 columns. In the below CDS view i want to do a count of the DMRGPOKEY column and then just get lines where count is more than 1. I don't know how to use the count because whenever I use it it's asking me to use count(distinct) and I need to get a count of that columns being repeated over several rows.

I tried searching but could not find anything on the internet.

Can you please help?

 

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDSV_GPOIDTRORG'

@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK

@EndUserText.label: 'GPO ID On Multiple Trade Org'

define view ZCDSV_GPOID_TRORG

as select from /vgm/gezstmit as A

inner join a910 as B

on B.hienr = A.zztradeorg

inner join konp as C

on C.knumh = B.knumh

inner join konh as D

on D.knumh = B.knumh

inner join /irm/gmlmbr as E

on E.msnum = D.kosrt

and E.kunnr = B.irm_kunnr

inner join /irm/gmlhdr as F

on F.msnum = D.kosrt

{

 

key A.zgpo_srcnum as gpo_source,

key B.hienr as hienr,

key B.irm_kunnr as irm_kunnr,

key E.zmbkey as ZMBKEY,

key B.datbi as DATBI,

concat(B.irm_kunnr,E.zmbkey) as DMRGPOKEY,

count(distinct E.zmbkey) as NumOccurrences,

B.datab as datab,

D.kosrt as KOSRT

 

} where B.kschl = 'ZMBR'

and B.kappl = 'V'

and C.loevm_ko = ' '

--and E.zmbid1 = ' '

and F.mstyp = 'ZGPO'

and B.datbi >= $session.system_date

group by A.zgpo_srcnum,B.hienr,B.irm_kunnr,E.zmbkey,B.datbi,B.datab,D.kosrt

-- having count(distinct E.zmbkey) > 1

entry.title: Re: Change Offlinestore without sending a request
entry.content:
Instead of updating the offline store you should just run a download to sync the offline store to the backend and pickup the changes you sent via the online service.  Your download will ideally just do a delta sync and you can further just download the entity set that was updated via the online call versus updating all the entity sets.

The other option is the always write to offline but trigger an upload after the save to send to the backend right away.

entry.title: Parcel, LTL, FTL Processing with Advanced Shipping & Receiving in SAP Transportation Management
entry.content:
This blog post explains how companies (like OEMs) can process their outbound freight from their distribution centers where the Warehouse and Transportation functions are well integrated allowing a possibility to automate the Transportation Planning and Warehouse execution function.

During Transportation Planning, focus is high on standard Vs. express shipping conditions agreed with the customer and in turn deciding when and what to be shipped as Parcel or LTL/FTL. As we know, each of this impacts the delivery SLAs and Costs incurred.

With Advanced Shipping and Receiving (ASR) functionality warehousing and transportation, functions together seamlessly, to achieve key benefits like:

  • Facilitate and prioritize the Pick, Pack, Stage and Load operations for Express shipments
  • Avoid a situation where pallets prematurely occupy and block staging area space waiting for the transport to arrive.

What is Advanced Shipping and Receiving (ASR) 

Let us understand this process from the perspective of an OEM selling Machines, Tools, Equipments and Spare parts.

Dealers of this OEM places orders of different kinds, some are to replenish inventory which means large number of order lines for equipments, urgent orders with very few line items for spares and some orders which are mix of equipments and spares. 

OEM wants to keep the transportation cost as minimum as possible and normally negotiates rates with Parcel Companies and LTL/FTL Carriers. Thing to note here is LTL/FTL is palletized cargo where as Parcels are just loose cartons.

Shipping conditions are enforced by the Dealers while placing the order, normally Standard Shipping or Express Shipping (in case of urgency).

Parcel has its own definition on "Shipment Size and Weight threshold" as published by different service providers like FedEx, UPS, DPD, Postal companies etc. Which also means, not every Express Shipment is a Parcel Shipment.

OEM decides the best way to ship the order lines making sure that delivery is on time as also the cost is kept minimum.

 

Shipping Condition

Qualifies for Parcel 

Shipping Possibility 1Shipping Possibility 2ASR-Type
ExpressYesParcel Warehouse Driven
ExpressNoLTLFTLTransport Driven
StandardYesLTL ParcelTransport Driven
StandardNoLTLFTLTransport Driven

Technical Implementation:

  1. As demonstrated in the "process flow" below, Sales Order information is fed into the SAP TM Freight Unit Builder (FUB) to decide what can be shipped "Parcel" and what can go LTL/FTL. Invoking a BRF+ decision table Parcel related "Shipment Size and Weight Threshold" can be checked. (FUB) spits out Parcel and Non-Parcel Freight Units
  2. Parcel Carrier determination for the Parcel Freight Units is done based on the available Parcel Rate Contracts stored in the SAP TM.
  3. Express Parcels need immediate warehouse execution, such Freight Units are so identified as Warehouse Driven ASR scenario. Functionality of Delivery initiation for Unplanned Freight Units is used to initiate the Warehouse activitiesParcel & Non Parcel Process Flow
  4. Using automated consolidation jobs Non-Express Parcels and Non-Parcel Freight Units can be consolidated into LTL/FTL shipments and follow the Transport Driven ASR process. This avoids premature warehouse execution, which could block the warehouse capacity.
  5. Non-Express Parcel Freight Units which could not be shipped under consolidated LTL load can still be pushed as Parcel Warehouse driven process.
  6. Parcel Shipping Labels can be printed from the Packing Station in the Warehouse

Such end-to-end automated process running daily has a potential to increase the efficiency of the Distribution Center.

entry.title: Inter-company dropship process
entry.content:
Hello community

We are trying to design a process flow for the below scenario. It is a mix of Inter-company and Drop-ship.

Manufacturing Plant is in USA. Marketing Plant in UK. Customer is in Europe regions. Selling fully configurable vehicles.

Limitations: USA Manufacturing Plant will have the Variant Configuration, BOM, Routing, Costs etc. But UK Marketing Plant will not maintain any of these. They just order from USA.

Marketing plant enters the Sales Order (with UK Sales Area). The Plant on the line item will be USA. USA will manufacture and PGI the delivery, but UK Marketing Plant will need to receive it and add user manuals, alter a fender/other parts and then ship to European customer. UK Marketing plant will not stock the materials for long, they are more like receive, perform a couple of minor touch-up/changes and ship to customer.

We tried to map this flow but couldn't really succeed.

After the order is PGI'd in USA, we couldn't think of a way to ship to UK Marketing Plant without generating a billing document. Can we use 2 deliveries? or a virtual PO or some other way?

All suggestions are highly appreciated. Thanks!

SD (Sales and Distribution) SD Sales
